items.title: NFP +172k: 4-sigma beat resets the narrative — first positive 2-month revision in 10 months
items.idea: May payrolls came in at +172k vs median estimate +88k and above the highest estimate of +125k — a 4-sigma beat. March revised up to +214k (+29k); April revised to +179k (+64k). Combined revisions +93k — the first positive 2-month net revision in TEN months and largest since January 2025. 3-month average of NFP jumped from 79k to 188k, highest since February 2024. Average hourly earnings +0.3% m/m, +3.4% y/y. VIX surged +39.7% on the day.
items.angle: Every bank model built on a weakening labor market is now wrong. The Fed is closer to hiking than cutting. This is the pivotal print that changes the entire second half of 2026 narrative.
items.tweetA: NFP +172k. Median estimate: +88k. Highest estimate: +125k. This wasn't a beat — it was a completely different economy than the one Wall Street was pricing. First positive 2-month revision in TEN months. 3-month average jumped from 79k to 188k. Everything changes.
items.tweetB: The labor market didn't just beat — it rewrote the script. March revised to +214k. April revised to +179k. Combined revisions +93k. Every model for 'rate cuts by summer' needs to go in the trash. The Fed's next move is a hike, not a cut.

charts.desc: BMO chart of 3-month MA of payroll gains; DB chart of QCEW vs benchmark revisions.

items.title: BNP flips Fed call to HIKES — 10y to 4.75%, 30y to 5.35%, 2y to 4.35% by year-end
items.idea: BNP Paribas changed their Fed call from cuts to hikes, expecting the Fed to reverse all three 2025 insurance rate cuts at sequential meetings beginning December 2026 — 75bp of hikes through March 2027. New forecasts: 10y at 4.75%, 30y at 5.35%, 2y at 4.35%. The rise attributed equally to higher rate expectations and continued drift in term premiums, with fiscal risks not yet fully reflected.
items.angle: When a major bank changes from cuts to hikes, that is a regime call. If BNP is right, the entire duration trade inverts. Long TLT is a trap. The front end moves first.
items.tweetA: BNP just changed their Fed call from cuts to HIKES. They expect the Fed to reverse all three 2025 insurance cuts — starting December. 10y target: 4.75%. 30y target: 5.35%. Welcome to the second half of 2026.
items.tweetB: There are now major banks calling for Fed hikes. Not just 'no cuts.' Actual hikes. BNP sees 2y at 4.35% by year-end — 75bp of hikes through March 2027. $TLT is going to be a rough ride.

items.title: BAML/BMO/DB aligned: bear-flattener, pay 2y, target 4.60-4.75% on 10s — $119B supply wall this week
items.idea: BMO entered a tactical short in 10y at 4.45%, took half profit at 4.54%, targeting 4.60%. BAML recommends paying US 2Y and owning 1y2y inflation flattener. DB maintains 4.70%+ on 10s. June supply: $58B 3s Tuesday, $39B 10s Wednesday, $22B 30s Thursday. All three May auctions tailed modestly.
items.angle: The sell-side is aligned and the supply wall is massive. The auctions come right after CPI Wednesday.
items.tweetA: BMO entered a short in 10s at 4.45%, took half off at 4.54%, targeting 4.60%. BAML says pay US 2Y. DB says 4.70%+. The sell-side is aligned: yields go higher.
items.tweetB: $58B 3s Tuesday. $39B 10s Wednesday. $22B 30s Thursday. $119B in supply in three days — right after a 4-sigma NFP with hike pricing now in the market.

charts.desc: BMO yield chart with tactical short entries; June seasonal bias charts.

items.title: ING: Only 3 sectors created ALL US jobs since Dec 2022 — every other sector net negative for 3.5 years
items.idea: ING finds that leisure & hospitality (+70k), government (+52k), and private education/healthcare (+40k) created virtually all 172k May gains. Since December 2022, these three sectors have been responsible for 100% of cumulative US job creation. Manufacturing, technology, energy, retail, transport, financial services — all net negative over 3+ years. Wage growth is slowing, putting household finances under increasing pressure.
items.angle: This is the key crack in the 'strong labor market' narrative. The Fed is hiking into a labor market that is narrowing, not broadening.
items.tweetA: Buried in the jobs report: the only sectors creating jobs since December 2022 are leisure/hospitality, government, and education/healthcare. Manufacturing, tech, energy, retail, transport — all net NEGATIVE for 3.5 years. 172k is a three-sector mirage.
items.tweetB: 100% of US job creation since December 2022 came from 3 sectors. Every other sector lost jobs on balance. The headline hides a hollowing-out. The Fed is about to hike into a labor market that is narrowing, not broadening.

charts.desc: Cumulative job creation by sector since Dec 2022 — bar chart showing only 3 sectors positive, all others negative.

items.title: Warsh cherry-picking Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean: 92.1% r-squared to Core PCE with a 4-month lag
items.idea: Spectra's Brent Johnson: Fed Chair Warsh citing the Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean as evidence inflation is contained — but it is a lagging indicator with 92.1% r-squared to Core PCE when lagged by four months. Every single turning point: Core PCE moves first, Dallas Fed follows. This gives Warsh cover to hold even as real-time inflation data points toward hike territory.
items.angle: The Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean will confirm what Core PCE is already showing in 4 months. The market is underpricing the duration of this inflation surge.
items.tweetA: Warsh is cherry-picking the Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean to argue inflation is contained. Problem: 92.1% r-squared between Core PCE today and Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean in four months. Core PCE leads. Dallas follows. He is using the lag as political cover.
items.tweetB: Every single turning point in US inflation: Core PCE moved first, Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean followed four months later. Warsh knows this. He is choosing the lagging indicator to justify holding rates while real-time data screams hike.

charts.desc: Scatter plot Core PCE today vs Dallas Fed Trimmed Mean +4 months, r-squared 92.1%.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: McElligott's 'spot up, vol up' mechanic: why the AI rally reversal was always going to be violent
items.idea: Charlie McElligott (Nomura) warned the AI rally had become dependent on a 'spot up, vol up' dynamic: investors buying calls forced dealers to buy underlying shares to hedge — pushing prices higher, encouraging more call buying, forcing more dealer buying. Self-reinforcing. When the reversal began, same gears ran in reverse: lower prices forced dealers to sell, systematic strategies reduced exposure, risk models triggered. A mechanical cascade, less about emotion, more about structure.
items.angle: Understanding the delta hedging loop is crucial for timing re-entry — forced selling must exhaust before value bids can establish.
items.tweetA: McElligott's explanation for Friday: investors buying calls → dealers forced to buy shares to hedge → higher prices → more calls → more dealer buying. Self-reinforcing. Perfect until it isn't. Then same gears reverse. That was Friday.
items.tweetB: The AI rally wasn't just fundamentals. A huge portion of the advance was mechanical: dealer delta hedging of crowded call options. Nothing fundamental changed — the plumbing reversed direction.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman Pasquariello: 'healthy reset' — HF exposure, CTA length, momentum crowding all at extremes
items.idea: Goldman's Tony Pasquariello: selloff was a positioning reset driven by extreme hedge fund exposure, CTA length, and crowded momentum trades — not a fundamental change. The market absorbed the largest equity capital raise in US history without breaking stride. Retail investors still buying. Corporate buybacks aggressive. Structural bid intact. Collapsing correlations and expanding sector dispersion are the new regime signals.
items.angle: Future gains will come with sharper rotations and air pockets, not the smooth tape of Q1. Position sizing matters more now.
items.tweetA: Goldman's Pasquariello said it plainly: this was a positioning reset, not a fundamental break. Hedge fund exposure, CTA length, momentum crowding — all at extremes. The AI bull market thesis hasn't changed. The luggage just needed to lighten.
items.tweetB: The market absorbed the largest equity capital raise in US history without breaking. Retail still buying. Buybacks running. What you saw Friday was crowding clearing. Dispersion is rising — pick stocks, not index.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman Garrett: options market was raising insurance premiums weeks before equities cracked
items.idea: Goldman's Brian Garrett: implied volatility had been quietly climbing across the curve even as realized vol stayed subdued and indices ground higher. Downside protection steadily becoming more expensive relative to upside. Longer-dated vol firming. Tech and semiconductor vol began moving higher mid-week. Even energy vol was being bid on Iran tensions. The divergence between expensive hedges and calm realized vol was the tell.
items.angle: When implied vol detaches from realized vol on a multi-week basis, someone with information is buying protection. This pattern appears before most major dislocations.
items.tweetA: Goldman's Garrett: implied vol was climbing for weeks while realized vol stayed suppressed and stocks ground higher. The insurance market was raising premiums while the public thought skies were clear. Options traders knew what equity investors didn't.
items.tweetB: Downside protection more expensive relative to upside. Longer-dated vol firming. Semis vol rising mid-week. None alarming individually. Together? The clearest warning available before Friday.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Korea: foreign investors sold for weeks while domestic retail piled in on margin — then it collapsed
items.idea: South Korea became the epicenter of AI infrastructure investment. Foreign investors aggressively selling Korean equities for weeks while domestic retail continued buying with extraordinary enthusiasm, expanding margin debt and risk tolerance. When the reversal arrived, leveraged Korean equity products suffered collapses that 'immediately brought back memories of previous volatility accidents.'
items.angle: This is textbook thematic rally exhaustion: experienced money exits quietly, late momentum enters on leverage. The Korea divergence is a case study in how to identify when smart money is leaving before the narrative breaks.
items.tweetA: South Korea was the most crowded AI trade — memory, semis, the full stack. Foreign investors sold for weeks while domestic retail piled in on margin. The narrative held. Then it didn't. Leveraged Korean products collapsed in ways that brought back memories of previous vol accidents.
items.tweetB: Foreign capital left Korea for weeks while domestic retail was buying. Classic signature of thematic rally exhaustion: sophisticated exits, late momentum enters on leverage. This pattern appears near the end of every major thematic run.

items.title: $390B crypto cap wiped; $4.4B ETF outflows over 13 sessions; worst week since FTX collapse
items.idea: $390 billion in crypto market cap lost, $1.5-1.6 billion in long liquidations in a single rout, $4.4 billion in BTC/ETH/SOL/XRP ETF outflows over 13 sessions. Worst weekly rout since FTX. The ETF bid that was supposed to provide a floor turned into an exit door when traditional portfolio holders needed to raise cash.
items.angle: The ETF wrapper did not change the underlying volatility dynamics — it changed who was holding the bags. The 'institutionalization stabilizes crypto' thesis is dead.
items.tweetA: $390 billion wiped from crypto in one week. $4.4 billion in ETF outflows over 13 sessions. $1.5 billion in long liquidations in one day. This is what happens when Wall Street adopts an asset class but does not change the leverage mechanics underneath it.
items.tweetB: The ETF bid was supposed to be the stabilizing force. The data: $4.4B out over 13 sessions. Worst weekly rout since FTX. The ETF bid did not become a floor — it became an exit door when risk sentiment flipped. $BTC $ETH

items.title: BTC broke BELOW 200-week MA at $60,744 — RSI 15.4, -31.55% YTD, worst technical picture in years
items.idea: Bitcoin at $60,744, now below the 200-week moving average — a level that held every major correction since 2019. RSI at 15.4. 20% below 50-day SMA, 22.7% below 200-day SMA, -31.55% YTD. Digital Asset Market Clarity Act advanced in Senate 15-9 but BTC cascaded below 200-week MA anyway.
items.angle: If the 200-week MA fails to hold, this is a different kind of drawdown. RSI 15 on a weekly chart is historically a strong mean reversion signal. But until price reclaims the 200-week, risk remains elevated.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin just broke BELOW the 200-week moving average — a level that held every correction since 2019. RSI at 15.4. -31.55% YTD. The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act just passed the Senate Banking Committee 15-9. Good news + catastrophic technicals = the bid is gone.
items.tweetB: $BTC at $60,744. 20% below the 50SMA. 22.7% below the 200SMA. RSI 15.4. The 200-week MA held every major low for 5 years. This is the test. Either it holds and generates the most oversold bounce of the cycle, or this is a different kind of breakdown.

charts.desc: BTC weekly chart with 200-week MA and RSI.

items.title: CFTC approved US bitcoin perps — CME, Cboe, ICE sold off; the exchange fee-pool war has started
items.idea: CFTC opened a path for Kalshi bitcoin perpetual futures and gave Coinbase a regulated route through Deribit. CME, Cboe, ICE, Nasdaq shares sold off as investors priced risk that perps migrate beyond crypto into equities and indexes. These incumbents earn revenue from venue control, listing control, clearing control, and trading-hour control — perps and 24/7 on-chain venues attack all four. ICE is now studying Hyperliquid rather than dismissing it.
items.angle: This is the innovator's dilemma for exchange infrastructure in real time.
items.tweetA: CFTC approved US bitcoin perpetual futures. Kalshi + Coinbase (via Deribit) now have a regulated path. CME, Cboe, ICE sold off — because perps do not stop at crypto. 24/7 equity index perps are the real threat to legacy exchange fee pools. $CME $ICE $CBOE
items.tweetB: ICE is studying Hyperliquid. CME is moving toward 24/7 crypto futures. Classic innovator's dilemma. The incumbents know the threat — racing to cannibalize themselves before someone does it for them.

items.title: JPMorgan, BofA, Citi, Wells Fargo building tokenized deposit network via The Clearing House — defense against stablecoins
items.idea: America's largest banks preparing a shared tokenized deposit network through The Clearing House because stablecoins threaten to move cash-like balances off bank balance sheets. The goal: preserve regulated deposits while offering 24/7 atomic settlement. The market structure bill is a turf settlement over who can issue cash substitutes and intermediate payments under federal blessing.
items.angle: This is the clearest institutional signal yet that stablecoins are a genuine deposit threat. The regulatory battle determines whether tokenized deposits or programmable stablecoins becomes the default cash layer.
items.tweetA: JPMorgan, BofA, Citi, Wells Fargo are building a tokenized deposit network through The Clearing House. Not because they love blockchain — because stablecoins are threatening to pull cash off their balance sheets. Defense disguised as innovation.
items.tweetB: The banks want blockchain without balance sheet leakage: tokenized deposits inside the regulated system. Crypto wants blockchain that breaks the regulated system. They cannot both win. The market structure bill is a turf map.

items.title: SpaceX IPO June 12: the spread between official price and on-chain pre-IPO perps will define on-chain credibility
items.idea: SpaceX IPO expected to price and begin trading June 12. This event will test whether crypto-native pre-IPO perp venues (Hyperliquid, Binance-linked) function as legitimate price-discovery mechanisms or thin speculative side markets. Watch the gap between the official IPO price, gray-market/tokenized pricing, and on-chain perp levels. Crypto market desks claiming retail is rotating from bitcoin into SpaceX IPO exposure.
items.angle: SpaceX is the most anticipated IPO in years. If Hyperliquid prices it accurately before the official market, that is a credibility-defining moment for on-chain venues.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO expected June 12. The real question is not the price — it is whether Hyperliquid pre-IPO perps show as a LEADING price discovery venue or just a thin side market. If the spread is tight and it moves first, Wall Street's 'weekend casino' framing is dead.
items.tweetB: Crypto desks say retail is rotating from Bitcoin into SpaceX IPO exposure. Watch the gap: official IPO price vs. gray-market tokenized pricing vs. Hyperliquid perp. That spread tells you whether on-chain venues have become real price discovery or not.

items.title: Gold -3.3%, silver -8.27% on NFP; gold lost 200DMA at $4,327; USD index tests 100 for 7th time in 12 months
items.idea: Friday's NFP triggered precious metals selloff: gold fell 3.3% to $4,327.57, breaking below 200-day moving average (still upward-sloping). Silver fell 8.27% but closed just above its 200DMA. US Dollar Index returned to test the critical 100 resistance — seven times in the past year, failing to break above it each time. Key support zone for gold: $4,300-$4,600. Gold oversold on Williams %R for 3 weeks within an uptrend.
items.angle: Gold is oversold within an uptrend — historically a setup for strong rebound. But the 7th test of USD 100 is the binary event. CPI Wednesday determines whether the dollar breaks through.
items.tweetA: Gold down 3.3% Friday. Silver down 8.27%. Gold lost its 200-day MA at $4,327. The USD index is testing 100 for the 7th time in 12 months — failing each time. If it finally breaks through, commodities take another leg down. If it fails again, that is the relief valve.
items.tweetB: Gold at $4,327. Below 200DMA. Williams %R oversold for 3 weeks within an uptrend. $4,300-$4,600 support zone has provided multiple bounces. The secular bull does not end at a single MA break. But the dollar at 100 is the thing to watch this week.

charts.desc: USD Dollar Index 7-test chart at 100 resistance; Gold with 200DMA and Williams %R.

items.title: Polymarket: 2026 US rate hike odds rising sharply — Iran energy costs + 4-sigma NFP driving the repricing
items.idea: Polymarket shows odds of a 2026 US rate hike have risen significantly over the past month, driven by Iran war-related energy costs and the 4-sigma NFP beat. No longer just 'no cuts' pricing — market pricing actual policy reversal. Year started with 4-6 cuts priced.
items.angle: Prediction markets tend to lag the bond market but lead mainstream media. When Polymarket hike odds are rising, the narrative shift to general public is 3-4 weeks behind.
items.tweetA: Polymarket odds of a 2026 US rate hike have risen sharply over the past month — driven by Iran energy costs and now a 4-sigma NFP. We are no longer pricing 'no cuts' — we are pricing policy reversal. The year started with 4-6 cuts priced. Do the duration math.
items.tweetB: The market came into 2026 expecting the Fed to cut 4-6 times. One strong jobs report later, the conversation shifted from 'when do they cut?' to 'do they hike?' BNP says yes. Barclays says ECB hike is certain. The global CB easing cycle is over.

charts.desc: Polymarket rate hike probability curve rising sharply.

items.title: SOXX sold off on 3x normal volume Friday — last identical event was October 10th (start of AI correction)
items.idea: SOXX semiconductor ETF sold off on almost three times its normal trading volume on Friday. The only previous comparable volume event was October 10th — which marked the beginning of a sustained AI/semiconductor correction that lasted several weeks. Bulls need to see 21-day EMA reclaimed. NVDA pulled back 6%+ to test 50DMA at $205, with 200DMA at $188.34 as the next key level.
items.angle: Volume is a conviction signal. 3x normal on a selloff means institutional holders are genuinely reducing positions. The October 10 analog is uncomfortable for anyone long semis.
items.tweetA: SOXX sold off on 3x normal volume Friday. The last time this happened was October 10th — which marked the beginning of the AI/semiconductor correction that lasted weeks. Watch the 21-day EMA for recovery confirmation. $SOXX $NVDA $AMD
items.tweetB: Volume does not lie. 3x normal on SOXX means institutional conviction, not panic. The October 10 analog: similar volume → several weeks of damage. Bull case: 21-day EMA reclaims fast. Bear case: same playbook repeats. $NVDA 200DMA at $188 is the key level below.

charts.desc: SOXX volume bar chart with October 10 comparison.

items.title: EEM up 14.85% YTD vs SPX +7.66% — emerging markets outperforming US by 700+ bps, multi-year thesis forming
items.idea: EEM up 14.85% YTD, outperforming US large-cap indices by more than 700 basis points. Remains in a strong uptrend. Key support $55-56 zone. SixSigmaCapital note explicitly states this 'could be setting up for multi-year outperformance' and adds: 'I would not expect it to trade as low as $55 again unless there are significant disturbances in the Middle East.'
items.angle: Dollar weakness is the prerequisite. If USD finally breaks below 100 decisively, EEM reaccelerates. The combination of cheap valuations, commodity exposure, and potentially reversing dollar headwinds is the structural thesis.
items.tweetA: EEM is up 14.85% YTD, outperforming US equities by 700+ bps. That gap is not noise — it is a signal about where value actually is. While everyone fights over NVDA, emerging markets are quietly running. The dollar at 100 is the binary event for this trade.
items.tweetB: The conventional wisdom was: own US tech, avoid EM. One year later: EEM +14.85% vs SPX +7.66%. The 700bp outperformance gap is widening. I think this is the beginning of a multi-year EM outperformance cycle.

charts.desc: EEM vs SPX YTD performance chart.

items.title: V/MA/AXP at multi-decade cheap valuations — 23x fwd P/E with 14-19% EPS growth and fortress moats
items.idea: Visa at 23.22x fwd P/E with 14% projected EPS growth and ROIC that has roughly doubled in 9 years. Mastercard at 23.61x fwd P/E, ROIC in the 40s for half a decade, 232% card count growth in 13 years. American Express at 17.23x fwd P/E with 13% EPS growth. Price targets: V $575 (+17%), MA $372.50 (+15%), AXP $390 (+25%). Author debunks Citrini AI-agent routing threat: consumers want friction, dispute rights, fraud protection, float, rewards. BNPL runs on Visa/MC rails anyway.
items.angle: The Citrini AI-disruption thesis sent card stocks down 5-7% last month. If that thesis is wrong, these are the highest-quality compounders at the cheapest prices in a decade.
items.tweetA: Visa at 23.22x fwd P/E for a business growing EPS 14% with ROIC that doubled in 9 years. Mastercard at 23.61x with 40s ROIC for half a decade. Cheapest multiples in over a decade. Everyone is chasing semis. Nobody is looking at the payment tollbooths. $V $MA
items.tweetB: The Citrini 'AI agents route payments on Solana' thesis sent card stocks down 7% last month. Here is why it is wrong: consumers WANT friction — dispute rights, fraud protection, float, points. BNPL runs on Visa/MC rails anyway. The moat is psychological, not just technical. $V $MA $AXP

charts.desc: ROIC trend charts for V, MA, AXP; fwd P/E vs historical range.

items.title: Credit card delinquency at 13% — 15-year high; macro risk real even for pure network operators
items.idea: National credit card delinquency rates at roughly 13%, a 15-year high, while credit card debt continues rising. Elevated inflation outpacing wage growth, eroding household buffers. For pure network operators V/MA, direct risk is limited since they do not hold credit. But if delinquencies force consumers to reduce discretionary spending, transaction volumes will eventually show the strain.
items.angle: Monitor payment volumes vs. credit availability — that is where the V/MA thesis breaks or holds.
items.tweetA: Credit card delinquency rates are at 13% — a 15-year high. Card debt still rising. Inflation outpacing wages. V and MA do not hold credit risk, but if consumers rein in spending, volumes follow. Know which part of the payment stack you own.
items.tweetB: The V/MA bull case: they do not hold credit risk, banks do. True. But 13% delinquency is a leading indicator for consumer spending slowdown. The tollbooth still collects fees if cars pass through. The question is whether the traffic slows.

items.title: Citi: $5.5T tokenized securities market by 2030 — winners are custody/settlement/compliance, not the chains
items.idea: Citi projects tokenized securities market will grow to $5.5 trillion by 2030 as a base case, covering T-bills, public equities, money market funds, and ETFs becoming programmable collateral. The winners are the firms controlling settlement, recordkeeping, compliance wrappers, and institutional distribution, not the blockchain protocols with the best ideology.
items.angle: Buy the infrastructure, not the chains. Settlement and custody are defensible monopolies; permissionless protocols are not.
items.tweetA: Citi projects a $5.5 trillion tokenized securities market by 2030. The winners will not be the chains with the best ideology. They will be the firms controlling settlement, recordkeeping, compliance, and distribution. This is a custody war.
items.tweetB: Tokenization is the financial industry's next infrastructure battle. Citi's $5.5T map: T-bills, equities, money funds, ETFs as programmable collateral. Own the plumbing — settlement, recordkeeping, compliance.

items.title: Iran: US intercepts fresh attacks, peace talks stall — Barclays flags Strait of Hormuz oil tipping point
items.idea: Bloomberg reported US intercepted fresh Iranian attacks as peace talks stall. Barclays explicitly flags risk of 'tipping points in oil markets' as the Strait of Hormuz stays closed, noting geopolitics remain 'the dominant driver of the macro narrative.' Brent closed at $93.03 (-2.1% Friday) — strong dollar and demand fears outweighing supply risk for now. Barclays also says ECB hike is now 'all but certain' given higher euro area core inflation.
items.angle: Oil is being sold despite Hormuz closure because the strong dollar and demand fears dominate. But if peace talks completely fail, this trade reverses violently and fast.
items.tweetA: Barclays is explicitly flagging 'tipping points in oil markets' as the Strait of Hormuz stays closed. US just intercepted fresh Iranian attacks. Peace talks stalling. Brent at $93 and getting SOLD — strong dollar and demand fears winning for now. The asymmetry if that changes is enormous.
items.tweetB: Oil selling off despite Hormuz closed. The market is betting on a peace deal. US intercepts fresh Iranian attacks. Peace talks stall. Two things cannot both be true simultaneously. When the market reprices geopolitical risk in oil, the move will not be gradual.

items.title: Week ahead: CPI Wed (exp +4.2%), $119B Treasury supply, SpaceX IPO — most consequential week of 2026
items.idea: Wednesday: CPI May (expected +4.2% y/y, +0.5% m/m; core +0.3%) + $39B 10y auction immediately after. Tuesday: $58B 3y auction + NFIB. Thursday: PPI + $22B 30y auction. Friday: UMich Consumer Sentiment + SpaceX IPO expected. Wells Fargo expects May CPI at 4.2% y/y, driven by 8% surge in energy goods. ECB expected to begin tightening cycle.
items.angle: Any CPI upside surprise Wednesday goes directly into the Wednesday afternoon 10y auction. CPI then $39B 10y is the most important sequence of the week.
items.tweetA: The most consequential week of 2026 starts Monday. CPI Wednesday (exp +4.2% y/y). Then $39B 10y auction that same afternoon. PPI + $22B 30y Thursday. SpaceX IPO Friday. $119B in supply against a post-NFP market now pricing Fed hikes.
items.tweetB: CPI Wednesday. Consensus +4.2% y/y, +0.5% m/m. If it comes in hot, the 10y auction that afternoon becomes the most important market event of the month. The bond market is going to say something loud about fiscal sustainability and hike timing this week.

items.title: McKinsey: Space race redux, balancing tech budgets in AI era, real estate selective recovery
items.idea: McKinsey's weekly charts covers: (1) 'Space race redux' — commercial space economy data, timely with SpaceX IPO June 12; (2) 'Balancing tech budgets in the AI era' — how enterprises are reallocating IT spend as AI absorbs larger shares, with the implicit question of what existing spend gets cut; (3) 'Real estate's selective recovery' — which segments are recovering while others remain depressed. Primarily chart-driven.
items.angle: The tech budget piece is the most actionable: if enterprises reallocate to AI, legacy software categories face headwinds while AI-native infrastructure and APIs see tailwinds.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's 'Space Race Redux' chart lands the week of the SpaceX IPO. The commercial space economy is no longer just SpaceX — it is becoming a data visualization subject, which means institutional capital is starting to categorize it as an asset class.
items.tweetB: 'Balancing tech budgets in the AI era' is the most understated threat in enterprise software right now. It is not 'should we spend on AI' — it is 'what existing spend gets cut to fund it.' Legacy ERP, traditional CRM, redundant analytics. That is the question.

charts.desc: Space economy growth visualization; enterprise tech budget reallocation chart.

items.title: Hedge funds covered 39.4k short contracts in 30y bonds — first capitulation signal from extreme short position
items.idea: CFTC Commitment of Traders data shows hedge funds net short 30-year bonds at 159.9k contracts, down 39.4k from the previous week — meaningful short covering from a historically extreme short position. First sign of short capitulation in the long bond. BAML notes June seasonals favor lower 30y yield 65% of the time by -6bp on average, front-loaded to the first 10 days.
items.angle: Watch whether covering continues through the $22B 30y auction Thursday. If bears cover into the auction, that is a supply concession buy. If they re-short after, 5.00-5.10% holds as resistance.
items.tweetA: Hedge funds covered 39.4k short contracts in 30y bonds last week, down to net short 159.9k. First meaningful capitulation from an extreme short position. Doesn't mean bonds are a buy — but the most aggressive bears are taking chips off before $22B 30y supply Thursday.
items.tweetB: The 30y bond trade had one of the most extreme hedge fund short positions in years. They covered 39.4k contracts last week. Either they are wrong, or they are lightening up before Thursday's $22B 30y auction concession. Watch 5.00-5.10%.

charts.desc: Hedgopia CoT chart showing net positioning in 30y Treasury futures with short covering highlighted.
